Daily DAP Accessibility Report — 2026-04-16#

Run ID: run-2026-04-16-c36aeec95b · Status: success · Source data: 2026-04-14 · Generated: 2026-04-16T09:50:24.102Z

View HTML/CSS/JS Code Quality report for this date →

About These Reports#

The Digital Analytics Program (DAP) is a U.S. government analytics service that collects website traffic data across participating federal agencies. DAP tracks page views, visitor counts, and usage patterns for hundreds of government websites, providing transparency into how the public engages with federal digital services. The traffic data is publicly available at analytics.usa.gov.

This report measures the quality and accessibility of the top 100 most-visited U.S. government URLs as reported by DAP. Each day, Lighthouse scans are run against these URLs to measure:

  • Performance - How fast pages load for users (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Accessibility - How well pages work for users with disabilities, following WCAG guidelines (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Best Practices - Whether pages follow modern web development standards (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • SEO - How well pages are optimized for search engines (scores 0-100, higher is better)

Accessibility findings come from axe-core, the industry-standard accessibility testing engine embedded in Lighthouse. The axe findings surface specific WCAG violations such as missing alternative text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels that make government websites harder to use for people with disabilities.

Traffic data reflects daily visitor counts from DAP. URLs are ranked by page load count, ensuring the most-used government pages are prioritized for quality measurement.

Accessibility Trend Narrative#

Over the past 30 days of data (2026-03-17 to 2026-04-15), government website accessibility scores have improved (+0.98 points, from 91.29 to 92.27). Performance scores have declined (-2.63 points, from 54.43 to 51.8).

Today's aggregate accessibility score of 92.49 reflects the mean Lighthouse accessibility score across 99 successfully scanned government URLs. A score above 90 indicates generally strong compliance with WCAG automated checks, though manual testing is always recommended to fully assess accessibility.

Common Accessibility Issues (Top 10)#

The following axe-core rules were most frequently violated across scanned URLs today. These patterns indicate systemic accessibility barriers present across multiple government websites.

Human impact narratives for top issues

The following narratives describe the real-world impact of each accessibility barrier on people with disabilities. These barriers are not abstract technical failures; they prevent citizens from independently accessing government services.

target-size: Digital Motor Access

Small touch targets act as a digital gatekeeper, excluding individuals with tremors, arthritis, or limited dexterity from accessing essential services independently. These technical failures transform a routine interaction into a source of failure, stripping away the autonomy of citizens who require a frictionless, accessible interface to participate in digital life. The approximately 58 million Americans with ambulatory or self-care disabilities are disproportionately impacted by inadequate touch target sizing on government mobile websites.

Affected groups:

  • People with Parkinson's disease, arthritis, or hand tremors
  • Older adults with reduced fine motor control
  • People with motor disabilities using alternative pointing devices
  • People in situational impairment contexts (e.g., commuting, holding a child)

landmark-one-main: Primary Content Identification

Without a main landmark, screen reader users lose their most efficient way to skip to the primary content of a government page. Every page visit requires tabbing through the entire navigation structure to reach the actual content, a significant time and effort burden that accumulates across repeated visits to government websites for citizens managing ongoing benefit cases or legal matters.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

heading-order: Document Structure Navigation

Screen reader users navigate complex government websites primarily through heading structure, using headings as a table of contents to jump between sections. Skipped heading levels break the logical document outline, causing confusion about the hierarchy of information and forcing users to re-read sections to understand the relationship between topics, adding significant time and effort to information-gathering tasks.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

color-contrast: Visual Information Access

Low contrast text is one of the most pervasive barriers on government websites, rendering critical information invisible to the approximately 26 million Americans with low vision or color blindness. When agency announcements, form instructions, error messages, or legal notices are displayed in insufficient contrast, affected citizens are denied equal access to the information they need to exercise their rights and access public services.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision including age-related vision loss
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

link-name: Link Purpose Clarity

Links without accessible names are completely useless to screen reader users who navigate government pages by jumping between links. An unnamed link could lead anywhere, and activating it unknowingly could trigger downloads, open unexpected pages, or initiate unintended processes. On government websites, unnamed links undermine the informed consent principle by preventing citizens from knowing where a link will take them.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

label-content-name-mismatch: Voice Control Activation

When the accessible name of an element does not match its visible text, voice control users cannot activate it using the words they see on screen. Saying "click Submit" to a button whose visual label is "Submit" but whose accessible name is "submitbtn" results in no action. This mismatch silently breaks voice control for citizens with motor disabilities who depend on this input method to interact with government websites.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People with low vision relying on screen magnifiers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using voice control software

html-has-lang: Language Identification

Without a language attribute, screen readers default to the user's system language settings, which may cause government content to be read in the wrong language with incorrect pronunciation. This is particularly harmful for multilingual citizens accessing government services in their non-primary language, and for users of braille displays that depend on language metadata for proper rendering.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with cognitive or language differences

listitem: List Item Semantic Context

Orphaned list items outside of list containers lose their semantic meaning and list navigation properties. Screen reader users rely on list structure to understand that a set of items is related and to count the total number of items. Government navigation menus, step lists, and option groups presented as invalid lists are harder to parse and navigate efficiently.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities navigating by keyboard

aria-required-children: Widget Structure Integrity

ARIA composite widgets without the correct child roles create structurally broken interfaces for screen reader users. Menus without menu items, lists without list items, and grids without cells all degrade to incoherent structures, making navigation impossible and forcing blind users to abandon complex interactive government tools.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities navigating by keyboard

aria-required-parent: Widget Context Integrity

ARIA elements without their required parent context lose semantic meaning for screen readers. A list item without a list, or a tab panel without a tab list, strips away the structural context that screen reader users depend on to understand and navigate government websites. This disorientation forces repeated and often futile navigation attempts.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities navigating by keyboard
Disability icon key
Without Vision — People who are blind or have no functional vision (1.0% of U.S. population — ~3,400,000 Americans)
Limited Vision — People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast (2.4% of U.S. population — ~8,100,000 Americans)
Without Perception of Color — People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness) (4.3% of U.S. population — ~14,500,000 Americans)
Without Hearing — People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content (0.3% of U.S. population — ~1,100,000 Americans)
Limited Hearing — People with hearing loss who may struggle with audio without accommodations (3.5% of U.S. population — ~11,900,000 Americans)
Without Speech — People who cannot use speech or voice-based input effectively (0.5% of U.S. population — ~1,700,000 Americans)
Limited Manipulation — People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity (2.2% of U.S. population — ~7,600,000 Americans)
Limited Reach and Strength — People with limited reach, strength, or stamina (5.8% of U.S. population — ~19,600,000 Americans)
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — People with cognitive, learning, or language differences (4.7% of U.S. population — ~15,900,000 Americans)

These icons show which groups of people with disabilities are excluded by each accessibility barrier. Where page view data is available, each icon shows an estimated number of people potentially excluded (page loads for affected URLs × disability prevalence rate from U.S. Census ACS 2023). Hover over or focus an icon to see the full estimate and methodology. Icons follow the Section 508 Functional Performance Criteria and the equivalent EU EN 301 549 v3.2.1 Table B.2 categories.

Prevalence data: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; supplemental data from CDC, NIDCD, AFB, and NIH/NEI. U.S. population base: ~336,500,000 (2023 estimate). Reviewed annually.

See the Section 508 WCAG to FPC mapping for additional detail on how accessibility requirements map to functional needs.

Usability Heuristics Summary#

The most common accessibility violations map to Nielsen Norman Group's 10 usability heuristics. This table shows which heuristics are most affected by today's top axe-core violations, helping to identify where systemic usability barriers exist.

Americans Excluded by Disability Today#

Based on 71,561,147 page loads across successfully scanned government URLs and U.S. disability prevalence rates from the U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101 (2023), an estimated 5,120,000 Americans encountered an accessibility barrier on a government website today.

Each row shows the number of people in a disability group who visited a page with at least one accessibility issue that affects their group. Prevalence rates and U.S. population estimates are derived from the American Community Survey (ACS) and supplemental sources. These figures are rough estimates intended to illustrate the scale of accessibility barriers.

Estimated Americans excluded today by disability category (2023 Census data)
Icon Disability Group (Section 508 FPC) U.S. Prevalence U.S. Population Affected Page Loads with Barriers Est. Americans Excluded Today

Limited Manipulation 2.2% ~7,600,000 43,900,000 960,000
Without Perception of Color 4.3% ~14,500,000 19,900,000 850,000
Limited Vision 2.4% ~8,100,000 26,700,000 640,000
Without Vision 1.0% ~3,400,000 45,100,000 450,000
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities 4.7% ~15,900,000 7,100,000 330,000
Without Hearing 0.3% ~1,100,000 45,100,000 130,000

Sources: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; disability prevalence from ACS 2023 and supplemental CDC/NIDCD/NIH data. Reviewed annually.

Performance Impact on Americans#

Google defines a good Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) as under 2.5 seconds and recommends pages under 1.6 MB. The figures below estimate how much extra time Americans spend waiting, and how much extra data is transferred, because government websites fall short of these benchmarks. Calculations are based on 93 successfully scanned URLs with Lighthouse timing data.

Estimated performance impact vs. Google benchmarks for government URLs scanned on April 16, 2026
Metric Estimated total (April 16, 2026) Notes
Extra time waiting (vs 2.5s LCP benchmark) 11 years, 20 daysExtra time is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual LCP − 2.5s) × page loads. Total: 348,899,981 seconds. 348,899,981 seconds
Extra data transferred (vs 1.6 MB page weight benchmark) ~76.3 TB (3,175 copies of Wikipedia)Extra data is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual page weight − 1.6 MB) × page loads. Across 93 URLs with weight data

LCP and page weight are measured by Lighthouse. Wikipedia copy count uses a size of 24.05 GB per Wikipedia:Size of Wikipedia. These are rough estimates based on a sample of the top government URLs by traffic. Hover or focus the estimated totals for calculation details.

Content Density (Words per Megabyte)#

Words-per-Megabyte (WpM) measures how efficiently a page delivers readable content relative to its total download size. A low ratio indicates Digital Bloat—the page transfers far more data than the text it contains. Pages below 200 WpM are flagged for optimization review.

  • URLs with content metrics: 69
  • Average word count: 234
  • Average Words/MB: 127
  • URLs below 200 WpM threshold: 57
Content density for top URLs scanned on April 16, 2026 (up to 10 shown)

https://studentaid.gov 13 3

Word counts are extracted from the page’s main article content using Mozilla Readability, which strips navigation, sidebars, and ads. Page weight is the total resource size measured by Lighthouse. Pages below 200 WpM may impose unnecessary data costs on users on metered connections.

Detected Technologies#

Technology signals detected from the network requests loaded by each scanned URL. CMS detection identifies WordPress, Drupal, and Joomla from characteristic asset paths. USWDS detection identifies use of the U.S. Web Design System.

Drupal29Sites: acf.gov, acquisition.gov, archives.gov, cbp.gov, cisa.gov, cms.gov, dea.gov, epa.gov, faa.gov, fdic.gov, fisheries.noaa.gov, fns.usda.gov, fs.usda.gov, gsa.gov, home.treasury.gov, justice.gov, medicare.gov, ncei.noaa.gov, nhlbi.nih.gov, noaa.gov, ofac.treasury.gov, osha.gov, sam.gov, studentaid.gov, tsa.gov, usa.gov, uscis.gov, usgs.gov, uspto.gov29%

USWDS detected on 34 of 99 scanned URLs (34%).

Observed USWDS versions: 2.9.0Sites: fbi.gov, 3.8.0Sites: fdic.gov. The latest release is available at github.com/uswds/uswds/releases.

Third-Party JavaScript Services#

Third-party scripts detected from network requests loaded by scanned pages. These services may affect page performance, user privacy, and security posture.

12 services marked ⚠ tracking send user data to third-party servers and may have privacy implications for site visitors.

Accessibility Statements (M-24-08)#

OMB Memorandum M-24-08 requires each federal agency to publish a digital accessibility statement that includes contact information for accessibility issues, known limitations, and a link to the agency Section 508 program page.

61 of 99 domains (62%) checked have a detectable accessibility statement at a standard URL path.

Required Federal Links (M-17-06)#

OMB Memorandum M-17-06 and the 21st Century IDEA Act require federal agency public websites to provide links to a Privacy Policy, Contact page, and FOIA page. These were among the compliance criteria benchmarked by the performance.gov federal website performance initiative (no longer active, but the underlying requirements remain in force).

59 of 99 domains (60%) have all three required link types detectable at standard URL paths.

Section 508: Legal Requirements vs. Best Practices#

Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act was enacted in 1998 and requires U.S. federal agencies to make their electronic and information technology accessible to people with disabilities. The technical standards were significantly updated through the ICT Accessibility Standards refresh, which became effective in January 2018 — the same year Apple celebrated the 10th anniversary of the iPhone. That refresh incorporated WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published in 2008) as the baseline for web content.

The web has changed dramatically since then. What agencies are legally required to meet today is built on a standard from 2008 — the same era as the original iPhone. Agencies should treat WCAG 2.0 AA as the floor, not the ceiling.

Legal Requirement

WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published 2008)

Referenced by the Section 508 ICT Standards refresh (effective 2018). Agencies must meet these 38 success criteria to satisfy their Section 508 obligations for web content.

  • Text alternatives for non-text content
  • Captions and audio descriptions for multimedia
  • Keyboard accessibility and focus management
  • Minimum color contrast (4.5:1 for normal text)
  • No seizure-inducing content
  • Consistent navigation and labeling
  • Error identification and correction

Section 508 web content requirements (E205)

Best Practices

WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 Level AA (published 2018 and 2023)

These later versions add criteria that reflect how the web actually works today — mobile devices, cognitive accessibility, and modern authentication patterns — and are strongly recommended for any agency aiming to serve all Americans. The US Federal Government is one of the last to use the legacy WCAG 2.0 AA Standard, as it was developed ~2 decades ago.

  • WCAG 2.1 (2018): 17 new criteria for mobile, low vision, and cognitive accessibility
  • WCAG 2.2 (2023): 9 more criteria for authentication and cognitive tasks, removing the outdated "Parsing" criterion
  • W3C now recommends WCAG 2.2 as the current normative standard for new and updated content

What is new in WCAG 2.2 · WCAG 2.2 specification

The scores and findings in this report are based on automated checks aligned with WCAG 2.x rules surfaced by axe-core. Passing automated checks is necessary but not sufficient: manual testing with assistive technologies is required to verify true conformance and to catch barriers that automation cannot detect.

Take Action#

Today's scan identified 200 accessibility barriers across the most-visited U.S. government websites, affecting an estimated 5,120,000 Americans with disabilities. Here is how you can help.

  • Read the federal accessibility report. The 2025 Governmentwide Section 508 Assessment from the GSA Administrator details the state of federal accessibility compliance and the steps agencies are taking to improve.
  • Submit URLs you care about. Want specific government pages included in future scans? Submit them to the Open Scans project to help broaden coverage.
  • Test with free tools. Do your own automated and manual accessibility testing with Accessibility Insights and Google Lighthouse. Automated tools catch a significant portion of WCAG failures quickly; manual review with assistive technologies catches the rest.
  • Adopt and update the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS). The USWDS is already a strong foundation for accessible, consistent federal websites, but many agencies have not adopted it or are running outdated versions. Encourage your agency to adopt or upgrade so every American benefits from its accessibility work.
  • Hire people with disabilities. People with disabilities are the ultimate experts on digital barriers. Bringing them into design, engineering, and testing teams — not just as consultants but as full-time employees — leads to more accessible products and services for everyone.

Accessibility Findings

https://tools.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 10

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name) DAP-c26fc51b

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~98KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~98,569 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 9,856,920 affected page loads) ~29KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~29,571 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 9,856,920 affected page loads) ~216KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~216,852 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 9,856,920 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1 DAP-7ca376ec

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a#headingOneAnchor > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 2 DAP-958bc2d3

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a.collapsed > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 3 DAP-958bc2d3

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a.collapsed > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children) DAP-82af62ae

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Disabilities affected: ~98KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~98,569 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 9,856,920 affected page loads) ~29KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~29,571 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 9,856,920 affected page loads) ~216KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Element 1 DAP-4f198039

Element path: div#g-navigation > div.nav-full > nav > ul.nav-list

Snippet:

<ul class="nav-list" role="menubar">

Label: Skip Quick Tools Links Quick Tools Track a Package Informed Delivery …

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has children which are not allowed: li[tabindex]
`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent) DAP-b319271f

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Disabilities affected: ~98KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Element 1 DAP-b8ca2593

Element path: nav > ul.nav-list > li.qt-nav > a.nav-first-element

Snippet:

<a aria-expanded="false" role="menuitem" tabindex="0" aria-haspopup="true" class="nav-first-element menuitem" href="#">

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) DAP-8c4fed15

Label:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

  • Element has no title attribute
List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-dd951b79

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-e62a32c4

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt) DAP-d36929f4

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) DAP-896ffcb5

Accessibility Findings

https://usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 9

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) DAP-4bdf91e9

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~19KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~19,690 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,969,008 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~5,907 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 1,969,008 affected page loads) ~43KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~43,318 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 1,969,008 affected page loads)

Affected elements (4):

Element 1 DAP-1aacc3db

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li.active

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="0" aria-checked="true" class="active">

Label: Slide 1

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="true"

Element 2 DAP-19fe08e3

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="1" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 2

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="false"

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="2" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 3

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="false"

Element 4 DAP-19fe08e3

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="3" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 4

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="false"
Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children) DAP-29ea0262

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent) DAP-040ea114

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt) DAP-474d58d2

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~19KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~19,690 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,969,008 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) DAP-f1542b28

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-2be6feaa

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport) DAP-7b9fc94e

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~47KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~47,256 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,969,008 affected page loads)

Element 1 DAP-efeb19cc

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-c24efba6

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-aee85114

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Affected elements (2):

Element 1 DAP-a58acc88

Element path: div#1547960993749 > div#g-navigation > div.nav-full > a#endnav

Snippet:

<a name="endnav" id="endnav" href="#" class="hidden-skip">

Label:  

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element path: div#1547960993751 > div#global-footer--wrap > footer.global-footer > a.global-footer--logo-link

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.usps.com/" class="global-footer--logo-link">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-ad446192

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) DAP-86bc430e

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~33KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~33,639 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,401,638 affected page loads) ~60KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Element 1 DAP-91b3be38

Snippet:

<input id="inputstring" name="inputstring" type="text" value="Enter location ..." onclick="this.value=''" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-0d01a89e

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt) DAP-f0437918

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

  • Heading order invalid
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) DAP-817c69a4

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~23KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~23,415 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 975,644 affected page loads) ~41KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~41,953 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 975,644 affected page loads)

Element 1 DAP-25e0050e

Element path: div.fsa-banner-content-body > span > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="/loan-simulator">

Label: explore and apply for other repayment plans

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.64:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0000ee, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Accessibility Findings

https://travel.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 2

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport) DAP-99efae35

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~21KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~21,487 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 895,304 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-06ade13b

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) DAP-3e7fec37

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~8KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~8,669 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 866,911 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,601 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 866,911 affected page loads) ~19KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-2428d6f9

Element path: nav.mobile-navbar > div.mobile-navbar_top > div.d-flex > a#cdc-mobile-search-toggle

Snippet:

<a id="cdc-mobile-search-toggle" class="nav-link" href="#" aria-controls="cdc-mobile-search-form">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Skip links are not focusable. (rule: skip-link) DAP-b3698d5b

Including a skip link can help users skip to the main content to save time. Learn more about skip links.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-6eb16f7d

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 2

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-08b676ee

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Estimated ~8,568 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 856,821 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element 1 DAP-26dff29d

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Accessibility Findings

https://radar.weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 77

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-63fbd253

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Estimated ~33,409 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 710,838 affected page loads)

https://uscis.gov

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus) DAP-056620d5

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

https://justice.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list) DAP-5b842ec0

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Disabilities affected: ~6KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~6,022 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 602,164 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,806 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 602,164 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-6300334a

Element path: div > div.most-wanted-view > div.item-list > ul.slick-initialized

Snippet:

<ul class="slick-initialized slick-slider slick-dotted">

Label: Kun Jiang DEA Field Office: New York Southern Wanted For: Fentanyl importation …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • List element has direct children that are not allowed: div

Accessibility Findings

https://sec.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 98

Axe findings: 1

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-fa2dd510

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Estimated ~12,786 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 581,200 affected page loads)

https://nps.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 100

Axe findings: 1

Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch) DAP-83ee28e4

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Accessibility Findings

https://science.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 1

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-d11e6ced

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~5KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~5,014 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 501,429 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,504 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 501,429 affected page loads)

Affected elements (4):

Element 1 DAP-1f661c7a

Element path: header.hds-nasa-header > div.usa-nav-container > div.grid-col > button#global-navigation-trigger--mobile

Snippet:

<button id="global-navigation-trigger--mobile" class="hds-global-menu-toggle menu-toggle menu-mobile-toggle" aria-expanded="false" data-menu-action="open" data-menu-location="left">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-94f6c92a

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Element 1 DAP-13779943

Element path: aside#tsp-toolbar > ul > li > button#toggle-tsp-zoom

Snippet:

<button id="toggle-tsp-zoom" class="tooltip">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) DAP-c0c32991

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~11KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~11,381 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 474,193 affected page loads) ~20KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~20,390 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 474,193 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-dbbb92aa

Element path: div.usa-grid > div.usa-footer-logo > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.tsp.gov/contact/">

Label: contact us

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.5:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #d6d7d9, surrounding text: #aeb0b5)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Accessibility Findings

https://spc.noaa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 53

Axe findings: 8

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) DAP-9e537039

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~10KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~10,699 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 445,799 affected page loads) ~19KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~19,169 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 445,799 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1 DAP-b78616b0

Element path: ul > li > form > input#query-field

Snippet:

<input type="text" id="query-field" name="inputstring" size="12" value="Local Forecast by ZIP/City, State" onfocus="this.value='';" onblur="this.value='Local Forecast by ZIP/City, State';">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1

Element 2 DAP-6fc97dce

Element path: div.full_width > div.SPCWeatherHeadlinesBoxSlgt > div.SPCWeatherHeadlinesBody > font

Snippet:

<font color="#ff0000">

Label: Critical

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 3.53 (foreground color: #ff0000, background color: #f7f690, font size: 10.0pt (13.3333px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1

<font color="#ff0000">

Label: Critical

Screen reader users rely on frame titles to describe the contents of frames. Learn more about frame titles.

Element 1 DAP-d0cdbcfd

Snippet:

<iframe src="//www.facebook.com/plugins/likebox.php?href=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.facebook.com…" scrolling="yes" frameborder="1" style="border:none; overflow:hidden; width:500px; height:395px;" allowtransparency="true">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has no title attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-34fc29ea

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Estimated ~20,953 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 445,799 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-907c94fa

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="//www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt) DAP-84ac16d9

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) DAP-3f1e53d3

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) DAP-6b652fcf

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~9KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~9,808 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 445,799 affected page loads) ~25KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~25,856 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 445,799 affected page loads)

Element 1 DAP-3871b45d

Element path: div.SPCMain2 > div#midBlock > div#searchBox > a.SPC_twitter

Snippet:

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (20px by 20px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20px instead of at least 24px.

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main) DAP-485fce88

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,458 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 445,799 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,337 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 445,799 affected page loads) ~9KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~9,808 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 445,799 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-ef8f516e

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="//www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark
`<td>` elements in a large `<table>` do not have table headers. (rule: td-has-header) DAP-42964e6b

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that `<td>` elements in a large table (3 or more cells in width and height) have an associated table header may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about table headers.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,458 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 445,799 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,337 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 445,799 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-17306674

Element path: tbody > tr > td > table

Snippet:

<table cellspacing="1" cellpadding="2" border="0" width="100%">

Label: Hazard Thu (04/16) Fri (04/17) Sat (04/18) Sun (04/19) Mon (04/20) Tue (04/21) …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Some non-empty data cells do not have table headers

Accessibility Findings

https://reg.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) DAP-b71125e4

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~8KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~8,490 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 385,897 affected page loads) ~22KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~22,382 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 385,897 affected page loads)

Affected elements (15):

Element 1 DAP-6b8ed34e

Element path: div.row > div.col-xs-12 > sup > a#password-tip

Snippet:

<a role="button" id="password-tip" tabindex="0" data-placement="top" data-remotecontent="/entreg/assets/html/tooltips/tool-tip-forgot-useralias.html" data-toggle="popover" aria-label="Retrieve your username" data-bs-original-title="Retrieve your username">

Label: Retrieve your username

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 15px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 21px instead of at least 24px.

  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 21px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://fda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 2

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-ecc5de53

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,796 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 379,575 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,139 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 379,575 affected page loads) ~8KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~8,351 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 379,575 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-b98a7353

Element path: div.fda-ft-container > div.fda-ft-heading > div.fda-ft-heading-text > h3.fda-ft-heading-title

Snippet:

<h3 class="fda-ft-heading-title">

Label: Featured Topics

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-4d2e97e5

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,796 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 379,575 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,139 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 379,575 affected page loads) ~8KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~8,351 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 379,575 affected page loads)

Snippet:

Label: Skip to main content

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has a tabindex greater than 0

Accessibility Findings

https://medicare.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

`<frame>` or `<iframe>` elements do not have a title (rule: frame-title) DAP-9dcb4ced

Screen reader users rely on frame titles to describe the contents of frames. Learn more about frame titles.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,408 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 340,825 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,022 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 340,825 affected page loads) ~7KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~7,498 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 340,825 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-9ed2a763

Element path: div.QSIWebResponsive-creative-container-fade > div.QSIWebResponsiveDialog-Layout1-SI_6tbIx8xW9ShoDfU_content > div.QSIWebResponsiveDialog-Layout1-SI_6tbIx8xW9ShoDfU_embedded-target-container > iframe

Snippet:

<iframe class="" data-interceptid="SI_6tbIx8xW9ShoDfU" name="survey-iframe-SI_6tbIx8xW9ShoDfU" data-name="survey-iframe-SI_6tbIx8xW9ShoDfU" tabindex="0" style="width: 100%; height: 100%; border-width: medium; border-style: none;">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has no title attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Accessibility Findings

https://ecfr.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 89

Axe findings: 3

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) DAP-254a829c

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~6KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~6,814 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 283,914 affected page loads) ~12KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~12,208 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 283,914 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-23bad96b

Element path: div.container > div#main > div.main-title-bar > h1

Snippet:

<h1 class="">

Label: Request Access

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 3.12 (foreground color: #5797ce, background color: #ffffff, font size: 15.2pt (20.2496px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-5e8a0a02

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,839 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 283,914 affected page loads) ~852People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~852 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 283,914 affected page loads) ~13KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~13,344 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 283,914 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-75e88a3b

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main) DAP-c53ed398

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,839 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 283,914 affected page loads) ~852People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size because it is partially obscured (smallest space is 59.4px by 19.2px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 15.8px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://esta.cbp.dhs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 85

Axe findings: 4

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt) DAP-72867807

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,721 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 272,123 affected page loads) ~816People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~816 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 272,123 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-27009710

Element path: div.toggle-language > span.dropdown > button#languageDrop > img

Snippet:

<img _ngcontent-ng-c2420648736="" src="assets/images/flags/usUkFlag.png">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) DAP-4d2fde10

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~6KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~6,531 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 272,123 affected page loads) ~11KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~11,701 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 272,123 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-a5b23db0

Element path: div.col-xs-12 > ul > li#welcome.whoYouAre.1 > a.strong

Snippet:

<a tabindex="0" data-target="#visaModal" data-toggle="modal" class="strong esta-link" href="#">

Label: Visa Waiver Program

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.18:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #00416a, surrounding text: #333333)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list) DAP-1eb5adbc

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,721 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 272,123 affected page loads) ~816People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~816 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 272,123 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-d71436a1

Element path: div.container > div.row > div.col-xs-12 > ul

Snippet:

<ul _ngcontent-ng-c1794490273="">

Label: About ESTA and The Visa Waiver Program What is the difference between an ESTA …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • List element has direct children that are not allowed: div
List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-c3aa4ba1

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Accessibility Findings

https://my.uscis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 89

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) DAP-1c5b51c5

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,635 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 263,533 affected page loads) ~791People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~791 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 263,533 affected page loads) ~5KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. <a class="hamburger hamburger--squeeze" tabindex="0" type="button" id="toggle-sections" aria-expanded="false">

Label: Menu

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport) DAP-d2302c08

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~6KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~6,325 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 263,533 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-04858f4d

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,height=device-height,user-scalable=no,initial-scale=1.0…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 98

Axe findings: 1

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-b24ecdd2

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-b6e82c3e

Accessibility Findings

https://earthquake.usgs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-a0c91230

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,234 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 223,377 affected page loads) ~670People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~670 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 223,377 affected page loads) ~10KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~10,499 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 223,377 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-c2803bf6

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) DAP-4cf65fa2

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,962 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 165,084 affected page loads) ~7KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~7,099 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 165,084 affected page loads)

Element path: div.search > div.searchfield > form.search-title > label

Snippet:

<label for="q">

Label: search

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-d13c136b

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,651 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 165,084 affected page loads) ~495People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~495 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 165,084 affected page loads) ~7KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~7,759 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 165,084 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-8ebdad8f

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) DAP-d9636267

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,651 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 165,084 affected page loads) ~495People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. <a class="ver12MainLayout" href="/npspub/pub_cmn_login/index_live.jsp">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Fix any of the following:

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) DAP-4f595b4c

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-1572b804

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list) DAP-9dceef9b

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-4ead3bcb

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus) DAP-36bf93e1

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-0256ac01

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-ac217743

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name) DAP-f6cdbd99

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Accessibility Findings

https://fdic.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 82

Axe findings: 5

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) DAP-e081d0a5

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,305 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 130,546 affected page loads) ~392People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~392 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 130,546 affected page loads) ~2KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~2,872 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 130,546 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1 DAP-c581b385

Element path: div.field__item > div.paragraph > div.tabs-container > input#consumers

Snippet:

<input class="radiotab" name="tabs" type="radio" id="consumers" checked="checked" aria-expanded="false" data-once="tab-click">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"

Element 2 DAP-3d6a6d26

Element path: div.field__item > div.paragraph > div.tabs-container > input#bankers

Snippet:

<input class="radiotab" name="tabs" type="radio" id="bankers" aria-expanded="false" data-once="tab-click">

Snippet:

<input class="radiotab" name="tabs" type="radio" id="analysts" aria-expanded="false" data-once="tab-click">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"
Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-454ce981

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,305 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 130,546 affected page loads) ~392People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content.   • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-07fca181

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Estimated ~2,872 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 130,546 affected page loads)

Label: Skip to main content

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-b647a33a

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

  • Element has no title attribute

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) DAP-be681717

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Affected elements (4):

Element 1 DAP-6167e109

Element path: section.hero-section > div.hero-indicators > div.d-flex > button.indicator

Snippet:

<button class="indicator active" aria-label="Slide 1" data-slide="1">

Label: Slide 1

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 12px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16px instead of at least 24px.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-64fe4ab5

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-b47e8749

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://fs.usda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) DAP-dadcde85

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~2,611 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 118,674 affected page loads) ~6KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~6,883 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 118,674 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1 DAP-a9cc3d16

Element path: div.margin-y-1 > div.fs-site-branding > div.display-flex > a.font-body-md

Snippet:

<a class="font-body-md text-no-underline text-base-darker" href="https://www.usda.gov/" data-kmt="1">

Label: U.S.D.A

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (152.8px by 20.3px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20.4px instead of at least 24px.

Element 2 DAP-ff34f575

Element path: div.margin-x-0 > div.tablet:display-flex > div.grid-row > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.fs.usda.gov/about-agency/features" data-kmt="1">

Label: More Featured Stories

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (154.2px by 14.9px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 14.8px instead of at least 24px.

Element 3 DAP-2a0047ff

Element path: div.usa-footer__logo > div.fs-site-branding > div.display-flex > a.font-body-md

Snippet:

<a class="font-body-md text-no-underline text-base-darker" href="https://www.usda.gov/" data-kmt="1">

Label: U.S.D.A

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (152.8px by 20.3px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20.4px instead of at least 24px.

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 3

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) DAP-da02a1a5

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~2,846 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 118,591 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~5,099 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 118,591 affected page loads)

Accessibility Findings

https://usgs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus) DAP-267989d9

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,178 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 117,754 affected page loads) ~2KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~2,826 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 117,754 affected page loads) ~353People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~353 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 117,754 affected page loads) ~2KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~2,591 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 117,754 affected page loads)

Element 1 DAP-9b8080ec

Snippet:

<div class="grid-col-4 slick-slide" style="width: 254px;" data-slick-index="1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="0">

Label: VOLCANO Shishaldin YELLOW - ADVISORY 2026-04-15 18:18:10 (UTC)

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Focusable content should have tabindex="-1" or be removed from the DOM
Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-703deda8

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) DAP-e95a9017

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.
